title: Collection Station
html:
Jiang Wenxu steps out of the doorway and follows Song Yu east.

The reasoning is straightforward enough: the staff figure to the west is stationary now but that category of problem doesn't improve with time. Song Yu has 2 hours of layout intel on this building. The administrator's office theory is probably wrong, but Song Yu knowing the east wing well enough to be wrong about it is still more information than Jiang Wenxu has about anything west of 7-C. He checks the wall clock through the door of 7-C as he passes: 8:27 AM.

The painted line is a single white stripe, wide enough that both feet cross it at the same time. The light on the far side is what he'd observed from the door gap: same fixtures, a gray cast pressed down across everything that isn't lower brightness so much as a different temperature of light, the kind that makes surfaces look as though they've been washed too many times. The low sound he'd noticed in the corridor is louder here and directional now, coming from further down and to the right. Song Yu doesn't glance back to check if Jiang Wenxu is behind him.

The first staff figure is 14 meters ahead, standing in the center of the corridor with its back to them.

Song Yu dropped his voice to just above inaudible. "Second one is at the bend, 28 meters. After the bend there's 8 meters clear before the third." A pause as he watched the figure. "Third is by the administrator's door. Keep to the left wall, don't look at them directly."

Jiang Wenxu looked left. The doors along this wall had no red tape on their handles, just plain handles in the same institutional hardware as 7-C, each one with a label above it in the same format. He read the nearest as they passed: 8-A. All of the east-wing doors they'd passed were untaped, and item 7 applied to east-wing doors specifically. If the 30-second rule functioned as he suspected it did, based on the nurse-figure holding his door for exactly 32 seconds and walking away, then knocking on any of these doors and counting to 30 might produce the same result. He didn't know what result. But the nurse-figure had not killed him, and the 30-second interval appeared to matter to it, so he filed the observation and kept pace with Song Yu.

[They're IN the east wing. I thought he wasn't going to follow]

[The veteran with the pipe is leading and the new player is reading door labels as they walk past]

[Reading. Door labels. While the thing is RIGHT THERE]

[Something is genuinely wrong with this person]

[Or something is genuinely right with him. Not sure which]

At 12 meters out, the first staff figure turned.

Not toward them. It pivoted 90 degrees to face the right wall and stopped there, the motion smooth and unhurried in the way of something that doesn't need to check its own balance. The rotation brought the face briefly into their sightline for roughly 2 seconds before the profile disappeared behind the back of the head. The features were all present — that was the problem, not an absence but a placement. Each element was positioned close enough to where it should be that the wrongness landed after the image was already gone, like a figure in a photograph that looks correct until you try to describe it. Jiang Wenxu looked away and filed it under the same category as the finger joints and the neck proportions.

Song Yu had gone still against the left wall. He held up 2 fingers, pointed toward the figure, then made a sharp forward motion with his palm: go now, while its back is to the corridor.

Jiang Wenxu didn't move.

Item 1 said no loud noise or sudden movement in corridors after 10 PM and before 6 AM. It was currently 8:28 AM. Daytime. The nighttime bracket should mean the rule didn't apply right now. But Song Yu had said his first attempt at this corridor ended in failure and the only thing he'd done differently that time was run. Either item 1 applied during daylight with a reduced threshold, or sudden movement triggered something outside the item 1 framework entirely. He didn't have enough information to determine which, and the consequence for getting it wrong was whatever had ended Song Yu's first run before the bend.

Song Yu looked at him with an expression that said he had approximately 3 seconds before he walked east without him.

Jiang Wenxu held up one finger, looked at the nearest untaped door on the left wall — label above it: 8-B — and raised his fist and knocked 3 times. Not loud. The natural knock of someone who expects an answer.

Song Yu's expression closed off entirely.

The staff figure didn't react. Jiang Wenxu kept his eyes on it and counted in his head rather than on the door. At 22 seconds Song Yu shifted his weight and the sole of his shoe made a faint Squeak! against the linoleum; both of them froze and the figure held its position against the wall, and Jiang Wenxu resumed the count. At 30 seconds, the door handle turned from the inside and the door swung inward by approximately 15 cm. No figure stood in the gap. The room beyond was darker than the corridor, the light at a lower level than even the gray east-wing cast.

[THE DOOR JUST OPENED]

[Something opened it from inside]

[The staff figure still hasn't moved. Why hasn't it moved]

[Because he knocked. He KNOCKED and waited and then it opened and the thing didn't care]

[That's — is that what the sign means? Is that how item 7 is supposed to work??]

[The veteran looks like he's seeing a ghost and the new player looks like he's reading a spreadsheet]

He pushed the door open the rest of the way and went inside. The room was small, barely 4 meters across, with a single desk against the far wall and a row of shallow wall-mounted hooks above it. Most of the hooks were empty. One held a laminated card on a lanyard. The desk surface was clear except for a closed logbook and a key resting beside it, the key attached to a small tag by a short loop of wire. On the left wall, mounted at the same eye level as the WARD REGULATIONS sheet in his room, a sign read COLLECTION STATION in the same administrative font. Item 4: return all eating utensils to the collection station immediately after each meal.

He picked up the key and read the tag. Two lines, the same plain font as everything else in the building. The first line read FRONT DESK. The second read LOBBY.

'Not the same,' he thought.

Song Yu was still in the corridor. He hadn't entered the room, which was consistent with someone who'd spent 2 hours in this building developing a strong preference for keeping exits visible. Jiang Wenxu stepped back into the doorway and held up the key. Song Yu's eyes went to it, to the tag, to Jiang Wenxu's face.

"Lobby," Song Yu said quietly.

"Yes."

A pause. "The administrator's office isn't the front desk."

"Probably not."

Song Yu looked east along the corridor toward the second staff figure at the bend, then back at the key. Jiang Wenxu watched him recalculate 2 hours of planning in approximately 4 seconds, which was faster than expected. He filed this upward under Song Yu's competence column.

From the direction of the bend: the Scuff! of weight shifting, the doubled footstep cadence picking up a rhythm. The second staff figure had turned and was heading west. Song Yu clocked it immediately and made the same two-finger gesture, this time pointing west: back to the line.

They moved. Jiang Wenxu kept his pace at the upper limit of what could still be called walking, closing the 14 meters to the painted line in a way that produced the minimum possible sound from his shoes on the linoleum. Song Yu matched him without comment. They crossed the painted line and went directly to 7-C. Jiang Wenxu pushed the door open, held it, and Song Yu went through first.

The room was the same as he'd left it 9 minutes ago, the wall clock at 8:36, the east-wing smell stopped cleanly at the doorframe. Song Yu set his back against the wall beside the door with the pipe held loosely, and looked between the key and Jiang Wenxu with the expression of someone running a slower and more complicated version of the recalculation he'd done in the corridor.

"How did you know to knock," Song Yu said.

"Item 7," Jiang Wenxu replied.

"I read item 7." Song Yu's voice was flat. "It says don't open east-wing doors without knocking and waiting 30 seconds. It doesn't say anything about doors opening by themselves."

"The interval starts from the knock, not from when you open the door."

Song Yu was quiet for a moment. "And you got that from re-reading the sign."

"Yes."

Song Yu looked at the floor between them and said nothing. The silence had the particular quality of someone who had 2 prior instance clears and was currently in the same position as a person on their first.

[Someone is going to have to explain the last 10 minutes to me. Step by step. With diagrams.]

[New player finds key. Key says LOBBY. Veteran's whole plan is out the window.]

[He got the key by KNOCKING. He stood there and counted and KNOCKED and then it just gave him a key]

[The veteran looks like he wants to ask follow-up questions and also like he can't figure out how to ask them]

[Same, buddy. Same.]

The key resolved one question and opened a different one. He had a key tagged for the front desk and a front desk location confirmed to be in the lobby, and no knowledge of where the lobby was. The building layout he'd assembled from window observations and corridor time covered the 7-series rooms on the west side and the first stretch of the east wing. The lobby could be at the end of the west corridor. It could be accessible from a stairwell. It could be past the east-wing administrator's office that Song Yu had been trying to reach, which would mean Song Yu's route was not wrong, only incomplete. He checked the wall clock — 8:37 — and turned the key over in his hand.

"You've been further east than the bend," he said to Song Yu.

"Once. I reached the administrator's door before I had to come back."

"Is there signage past the bend. Anything on the walls."

Song Yu thought about it. "A fire evacuation map," he said. "On the wall past the second figure, right side. I didn't stop to read it."

An evacuation map would show the lobby. He looked at the door of 7-C.

"We go back," he said.

Song Yu didn't argue, which confirmed that his 2 prior clears had produced either a very high tolerance for plans that updated mid-execution, or a recent and involuntary education in the same. They went back through the painted line at 8:39, walked the 14 meters past the first staff figure without incident using the same pace as before, and kept left at the bend where the second figure stood with its back to the corridor wall. The map was where Song Yu had said it was: mounted in a frame on the right side of the corridor, the kind of laminated evacuation diagram that every institutional building puts up and no one reads. Jiang Wenxu read it.

The lobby was on the ground floor. This corridor was on the second. The stairwell access was at the western end of the building — west, not east — behind a fire door marked STAIRWELL B that would be, by the layout of the 7-series rooms, approximately 8 meters past the staff figure that had been standing with its back to the building since he'd first seen it through the window gap more than an hour ago.

Song Yu read the map over his shoulder. He said nothing for 3 seconds. "The whole east wing," he said quietly, "was the wrong direction."

"You got the layout," Jiang Wenxu said. "That was useful."

Song Yu looked at him to see if this was sarcasm. It wasn't. He seemed to determine this from Jiang Wenxu's face, which as a primary data source on his internal states remained almost entirely unreliable.

They retreated across the painted line and back to 7-C before the second figure completed another patrol cycle. 8:47 AM. Jiang Wenxu stood at the window gap. The staff figure at the west end of the corridor was still there, still with its back to the building, still motionless. He'd been watching it at intervals for over an hour. In that entire time it had not moved once.

The east-wing figures moved. They patrolled. They had rhythms, positions, predictable windows between them. The west-end figure had stood in one place since before he'd first looked through the boards, which meant either it was a different category of entity from the east-wing staff, or it was waiting for something specific, or the conditions that made the east-wing figures move simply hadn't occurred yet at the west end. He didn't have enough data to determine which.

He looked at the key. He looked at the door. Stairwell B was 8 meters past a figure that had been standing like a post for an hour and forty minutes, and the only thing between him and it was everything he didn't yet know about why it was standing still.
